text:
Working with APIs allows applications to communicate and share data. When making an API request, your program sends an HTTP request to a server. The server then processes this request and returns data, often in JSON format, which is easy for programs to read and manipulate.

Many APIs require authentication to ensure that only authorised users can access them. You typically need an API key, which you include in your request headers. Additionally, most APIs set rate limits to control how many requests you can make in a certain period. Exceeding these limits results in an error.

Proper error handling is essential when working with APIs. Your code should check the HTTP status codes returned by the server and manage potential errors like network issues or invalid responses.
